---
title: "ai-minerals"
---

```{=html}
<object data="data/derived/portfolio_charts/region_locator_map.svg" type="image/svg+xml" style="width:100%; max-width:1100px; display:block; margin:0 auto;" aria-label="Map of the project regions across North America. Each dot links to its chapter.">
  <img src="data/derived/portfolio_charts/region_locator_map.svg" alt="Map of project regions; dots are clickable in modern browsers." style="width:100%;">
</object>
```

I've spent nearly thirty years building anti-malware on Windows:
kernel drivers, Windows internals, low-level systems work. None of
that has anything to do with mining; this site is what I've been
playing with in my off-hours.

A few years ago I started helping my mom research a book about her
grandfather, Walter W. Johnson, a mining and mechanical engineer and
builder of large placer-mining machinery. In the process we
discovered that the family still owned a large interest in several
patented mining claims in Nome, Alaska. One of the claims had
recently been high-graded; the others purportedly still held unmined
gold. As I started researching the book and the mining claims, two
things happened that I was not expecting. 1) I fell in love with
mining. 2) Every mining problem I encountered looked like it could
be decomposed into a computer program. Computing Bear Cub's resource
estimates from 100-year-old drill logs is one example of that.

The rest of this site is me poking around to see how useful public
data is for machine learning at the scale of an individual
prospector or a family-owned claim. One offshoot of that line of
investigation is goldbug, which unites it with one of my other
hobbies: title research. goldbug pairs the ML signal with public
land law: it ranks the parcels you can legally stake, not the ones
already withdrawn, patented, or under active claim.

## Chapters

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [1. Bear Cub (Cape Nome placer Au)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)

A family-owned patented mining claim on the Third Beach Line at
Nome, staked in 1899 and last drilled in the 1910s-30s. I tracked
the 24 churn-drill logs down at the Huntington Library, built a
multi-engine OCR pipeline (Tesseract, TrOCR, Cloud Vision, Claude
Opus arbitrating with structured-output JSON) to ingest them,
mapped each hole onto a recent plat, built a 3D model of bedrock
and pay-zone, and computed Voronoi-cell volumetrics with Monte
Carlo uncertainty. Per-hole grades matched two mining engineers'
independent analyses within 6%. On top of the same grid, a
Gaussian-process recommender picks where hole #25 should go.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![Bear Cub aerial photo with pay-zone grade overlay](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch1_bear_cub.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)

[![Fence diagram: 24 Bear Cub drill holes, log-scale gold grade, pay-zone framing](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch1_bear_cub_fence.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)

[![Next-drill-site recommender on Bear Cub: max-variance + max-EI picks against the GP-predicted grade surface](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch1_bear_cub_gp.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [2. Regional MPM pipelines](regional.qmd)

For each of the four regions (Eastern Alaska porphyry-Cu, BC Golden
Triangle multi-deposit, California Mother Lode orogenic-Au, Arizona
porphyry-Cu), I built the same kind of pipeline: pull public USGS,
NRCan, and state-survey data onto a common grid, train a Random
Forest, and hold out spatial blocks during validation so the model
can't cheat by memorizing neighbors. The chapter walks through each
region's data, the model output, and which features it leaned on
most.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![Tanacross (eastern Alaska) porphyry-Cu prospectivity, random forest output](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch2_regional.png)](regional.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [3. goldbug (live tool, CA Mother Lode)](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/goldbug.html)

The Mother Lode model from Chapter 2 is interesting on its own,
but it doesn't tell a prospector where to actually go. goldbug
closes that gap. It takes the per-cell probabilities and filters
them to federal land that's actually open for an individual
prospector to file a notice of location on. The hard part isn't
the modeling. It's that BLM publishes the relevant data across
roughly a dozen different endpoints with inconsistent schemas,
and several of the obvious filters are subtly wrong. The chapter
has the live map embedded plus a writeup of what the tool does,
how the open-land calculation is built, and what it still doesn't
catch (historic mineral patents, split estate, placer-specific
modeling).
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![goldbug live map: topo basemap, federal gap parcels (yellow), active mining claims (red), with a parcel popup showing the score breakdown and the layer-toggle panel on the right](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch3_goldbug.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/goldbug.html)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [4. Placer-Au prospectivity (northern Sierra)](placer.qmd)

Placer prospectivity modeling is roughly a decade behind lode in the
published literature. The only production-grade transferable model is
the USGS Alaska watershed scorer. I ported it to the northern Sierra
deep-gravel belt at 250 m resolution, then added a supervised pass
(PU bagging + Random Forest + LightGBM + stacking + isotonic
calibration) on top, run as two separate classifiers because Tertiary
deep-gravel (pay strata on hillside benches above the modern drainage)
and Quaternary modern-channel placer have different geomorphic
signatures. The Phase 1 anchor-district gate passes 7 of 7 with a
held-out Lindgren PP73 secondary set matching the anchor median; v2
Tertiary stacking reaches OOF AUC 0.961 with the model-disagreement
between RF (0.995) and LightGBM (0.804) telling the leakage-care
story. The calibrated raster feeds the goldbug live tool for
per-claim sampling.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![Tertiary placer-Au prospectivity, percentile rank of calibrated probability](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch4_placer.png)](placer.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [5. Reproducing + auditing two published methods](reproductions.qmd)

I cloned the official code for two well-known mineral-prospectivity
papers and re-ran them with proper validation. **Lawley 2022**
(continental Zn-Pb, published AUC 0.983) reproduces cleanly, but the
published number includes a label leak (2.4 pp), a 1-D vs 2-D
spatial-blocking gap (9.0 pp), and a cross-continent transfer
collapse (~16 pp). Properly validated, the AUC drops to 0.71-0.87.
The published code also had a performance problem: a critical piece
used an O(N²) algorithm that didn't finish after half an hour. I
rewrote it as O(N) and got the runtime down to a few minutes.
**DEEP-SEAM** (Curnamona REE, published 86% top-2% capture) is
measured under a 0-D random split with no spatial blocking at all,
and the implementation diverges from the paper text in two places. I
tried to find a charitable read of each and report what I actually
saw.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![Waterfall chart of Lawley 2022 AUC stages](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch5_reproductions.png)](reproductions.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [6. Cross-region experiments](cross_region.qmd)

I ran a five-region transferability test on the DEEP-SEAM
deviation-network architecture. The 2-3× advantage over Random
Forest that the paper reports holds on its tuning dataset (Curnamona
REE, 7 positives) and nowhere else. The most pointed test was on the
US Western Carbonatite REE Belt (14 positives, the same deposit
type as Curnamona): DEEP-SEAM still loses to Random Forest. On the
other three regions (Tanacross porphyry-Cu, Arizona porphyry-Cu,
Lawley continental MVT under 2-D blocked CV), the gap widens to 7-37
percentage points in favor of tree-based methods. Useful negative
result.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![DEEP-SEAM vs Random Forest across 5 regions](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch6_cross_region.png)](cross_region.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [7. Drill planning under uncertainty](drill_planning.qmd)

A prospectivity map by itself doesn't tell you where to put the next
drill hole. I built a POMDP planner with Monte Carlo Tree Search,
run on the BC Golden Triangle regional prospectivity prior and
ported from Mern et al.'s Intelligent Prospector (GMD, 2023).
Three policies side-by-side on the same prior: random, greedy, and
POMCP. POMCP gains roughly 10-15% in expected reward over greedy
at modest planner-depth budgets, more at deeper budgets. This is a
1.0 version against the v1.0 assumptions (i.i.d. Bernoulli prior,
noiseless sensor); the v2.0 extensions (correlated draws, noisy
sensors) would widen the POMCP-vs-greedy gap.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![POMCP discovery curves on BC Golden Triangle](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch7_drill_planning.png)](drill_planning.qmd)
:::
::::

## Methodology

Keeping in mind that I am a programmer and not a statistician, here
is my take on methodology.

Federal and state surveys publish a lot of mineral data: occurrence
records, geologic maps, magnetics and gravity, stream-sediment
geochemistry, satellite imagery. I pull these onto a common grid and
each cell of land becomes a feature vector. I train a Random Forest
to recognize cells with known deposits, holding out spatial blocks
so the model can't cheat by memorizing neighbors. The output is a
per-cell score across the region: the model's guess at where the
unknown deposits are.

The part I've spent the most time on isn't the training, it's the
validation afterward. The methodology has four well-known traps:
inflated training positives, spatial leakage, exploration bias in
the labels, and cross-region transfer failure. All four show up in
this portfolio. Chapter 5 is the most explicit about quantifying
them, showing how much of two published AUC numbers each trap
accounts for. The same issues are flagged in the within-region work
where I noticed them, and I'm sure I missed some.

## Stack

Public data sources: USGS, NRCan, state surveys, Sentinel-2. Code is
Python: scikit-learn, pandas + pyarrow, geopandas + rasterio,
PyTorch (for the DEEP-SEAM port), PyVista for the Bear Cub 3D
visualization, Quarto for the site itself.

## Reproduce

```bash
git clone <this-repo>
cd ai-minerals
uv sync
quarto render
```

License: MIT.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
from datetime import date
print(f"\n---\n\n*Site rendered {date.today().isoformat()}.*")
```
